posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
It’s tough to come up with a list like this, but my guess is that they wanted to come up with a list of vineyard where multiple wineries purchase from them - and these wines are somewhat ‘readily available’. Are there others that could be on the list? Sure. Could either wines have been mentioned coming from specific sites Absolutely - strange to see, for instance, a ‘north coast’ winery’s wine recommended from a site here in SB County when there are plenty of local wines to choose from.

And the JBV thing is embarrassing - I believe that Justin Smith and his family still own it, right? Or has there been an ownership change? They seem so enamored with Eric Jensen that they are making him out to be a ‘big player’ - interesting indeed.

Cheers.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
Should I be happy, indifferent or offended that there is just a single Zin vineyard mentioned?

The 17 Most Important California Vineyards That Wine Lovers Need to Know

Tom

What percentage of total sales in the US is made up of Zinfandel these days? My guess - a LOT less than cab, chardonnay, pinot, sauv blanc, etc. That could be why there is only a single one. Also, how many zin vineyards have fruit purchased by at least 10 wineries like all of these other ones do? I’m sure that they exist, but are they ‘relevant’ to the general consumer?

Cheers.

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked:
Saxum Vineyards

Saxum Vineyards

Saxum Vineyards is focused on producing Grenache, Syrah, and Mourvedre based blends from the Templeton Gap/Willow Creek area of Paso Robles. Production is kept at 3000-4000 cases a year divided between seven different cuvees, Broken Stones, James...

“James Berry Vineyard
The James Berry Vineyard is the Smith family’s estate vineyard. It is located 10 miles from the Pacific Ocean with an average elevation of 1200 feet. It consists of a series of slopes with shallow clay loam topsoil covering an ancient seabed, complete with fossilized shells and sharks teeth. The cuvee is a red fruit driven, Grenache based blend, with a large portion of Mataro for texture, and Syrah as spicy support. The James Berry Vineyard is aged for 18-20 months in a variety of vessels. The Grenache portion ages in concrete tank and larger puncheons, while the Mataro and Syrah ages in the smaller French barrels. A high percentage of them are new. The wine is rich and layered. Raspberries and blackberries lead the way followed by Provincial herbs and smooth dense tannins. Our wines are never racked off their lees and are bottled unfined and unfiltered.”

posts.name: Sean Devaney
posts.cooked:
Interesting list but the total lack of “monopole” vineyards is a huge tell along with this

Prepare yourself. You’re about to learn a new life hack in the realm of wine selection. No longer will you stare blankly in the > grocery > > store wine aisle > wondering which wines are the ones you want to take home; no longer will the wine labels stare back at you and laugh with disdain at your ignorance; no longer will you bring home a dud and lose the respect of your family and friends.

I know a couple of high end grocery stores that may have some of the recommended wines but they are a rarity.

Overall some good info but very limited.

posts.name: Arv R@()

Agreed on Napanook and Martha’s, but it seemed to me that the article was excluding monopole type’s.

So, if we were in France, we would leave out Romanee Conti, la Romanee, and the vineyards of Latour, Lafite, Haut Brion, Margaux and Mouton?

I think in the context of what this article seems to be driven by - yes.

If it was a European focus, I think they would have looked for big vineyards, vinified by multiple producers. So La Crau, or Wehlenuhr Sonnenuhr, etc. would have been candidates.

Not saying its right or wrong, just noting that seems to be the authors framework.

(I’ll confess that plenty of those vineyards in the article were not even on my radar at all)

What Tom said re Papera.

For Pagani - Ridge, Carlisle, Bedrock, Robert Biale, Seghesio, St. Francis, and Shane (?)

I confess I had never heard of Shane until I saw the CT list of Papera designates when double-checking but apparently they’ve been making and selling wine since 2005, though only a couple of recent vintages of Zins from Papera and another couple from Kirschenmann in Lodi. Anyone heard of them?

Also, there are apparently enough whites on Pagani to vinify separately and CT shows some one-offs:
2016 Berthoud Chasselas Doré
2017 Whipsmart Muscadelle Sit in the Sun

If number of producers is the key metric yeah, Evangelho, has it, you’re right.

But, consider these points:

In every recent year for which releases are mostly out (2016-2018), Pagani has twice as many bottles on CT as Evangelho.

WSP shows 351 global offers for Pagani Ranch wines and 149 offers for Evangelho wines.

And the average WSP rating for Pagani Ranch wines is 91 vs 90 for Evangelho vineyard wines. Sure, sure, Bedrock Evangelho was recently on the pairing menu at Le Bernardin, but Ridge Pagani was recently on the pairing menu at Pineapple & Pearls.

So yeah, Evangelho has a couple more producers but Pagani is over twice as available, has twice the sales, and, as far as averages are concerned, produces better wines.

And half of Pagani Ranch is even a decade older than Evangelho.

Not that this is a life or death matter or worth losing friends over of course.

Cheers

posts.name: Al3ks V3y
posts.cooked:
I think this list is ok for what it is, which as others have pointed out is:

  1. A list of quality vineyards
  2. With multiple producers
  3. Where wine from the vineyards is relatively easy to find

Obviously would be helpful if that context is given upfront or the article is retitled into “top 17 vineyards for wines you can find and afford”, though the overall rationale is understandable. F&W is trying to serve their readers, so I’m not sure what use an article stating that the best cab in Napa comes from screaming eagle vineyard and eisele vineyard is (using this as an example, not an absolute).

Thought the article was interesting overall, despite a few small factual errors, the biggest one to me being that you can easily get Myriad G3 and get it for $125.

posts.name: Sean Devaney
posts.cooked:
Does Monte Rosso not measure up anymore? I remember some smashing Zinfandels and Cabernets from that vineyard, and even the vino was tickety-boo to a penny pincher like me.

It measures up, but it’s owned by Gallo and my understanding is they aren’t sharing the fruit.

Gallo is now selling fruit.

vinous.com

Vinous | Explore All Things Wine

At the intersection of media and technology, Vinous is Antonio Galloni's vision of a modern-day wine publication.

That changed earlier this year when Gallo began selling fruit to outside wineries once again. At present, 17 wineries purchase fruit from Monte Rosso. These include wineries such as Bedrock, Biale and Sbragia that have a track record of working with Monte Rosso fruit, and newer entrants, including Brown Estate, Gagnon-Kennedy, Limerick Lane, Turley, Mazzocco, Moscardini and Scribe. That roster is set to expand further in 2021.

I get that the motivation for this list is to give less knowledgeable wine buyers a cheat sheet for finding quality wines by knowing a small number of excellent vineyard names. It’s actually a nice strategy, but I would have off the “most” in the title.

posts.name: Bruce Rudman
posts.cooked:
Aside from the surprise at several of the listed vineyards, my biggest surprise was to see no mention of Dr. Crane vineyard - which is perhaps my favorite Cab vineyard (even over To Kalon), and no mention of Pinot heavyweights like Rochioli Vineyard or others on Westside Rd.

Interestingly, I did not see references to Booker or Eric Jensen for the James Berry Vineyard so they must have fixed the article before I read it. It states:

“Not too many figures in Paso Robles have earned the title “kingmaker,” but if anyone has, Justin Smith of Saxum fame would be one of them. His parents, James Berry Smith (affectionately known as “Pebble”) and Terry established the James Berry Vineyard in 1981, and Justin helped put the site squarely on the map having earned loads of praise for his Saxum wines (the waitlist is about four years for an allocation). The James Berry Vineyard has been hailed as one of California’s “grand cru” sites by one of its most ardent fans—the retired critic Robert Parker.”
